summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Back pain

History of Present Illness
The patient reports pain to palpation of the right lumbar spine. The patient reports decreased flexion and extension of the back. The patient reports a positive straight leg raise. The patient reports pain when bending over. The patient reports pain. The patient reports numbness. The patient has a history of discectomy.

Findings
Examination reveals pain in the left hand. The patient has been taking ibuprofen.

Assessment
The patient has back pain.

Plan
The doctor will refer the patient to physical therapy after the MRI results are back. The patient should continue to take tylenol. The doctor prescribed ultram 50 milligrams every four hours as needed. The doctor prescribed meloxicam 15 milligrams once a day.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Back pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Bryan Smith is a 55-year-old male with a past medical history significant for and prior discectomy, who presents with back pain.

The patient reports he felt something in the lower right side of his back while pushing a refrigerator up through another room. This happened about 5 days ago. The patient experiences pain while bending over. He has a history of a discectomy. He is worried that something happened. He has been taking ibuprofen, which has not been beneficial alone. With the combination of Tylenol and ibuprofen, he experiences symptomatic relief. He denies numbness and tingling in his legs, and any problems with his bladder or bowels.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain or dyspnea on exertion.
• Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath.
• Gastrointestinal: Denies hematemesis, hematochezia, melena, heartburn, or abdominal pain.
• Genitourinary: Denies urinary urgency, pain, or incontinence.
• Musculoskeletal: Endorses lower right side back pain.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs. No extra heart sounds.
• Musculoskeletal: Pain to palpation to the right lumbar spine and the paraspinal muscles. Decreased flexion and extension of the back. Positive straight leg raise on the right. Strength is good bilaterally in the lower extremities.

RESULTS

X-ray of the lumbar spine is unremarkable. Normal bony alignment. No fractures were noted.

Labs: Within normal limits.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Bryan Smith is a 55-year-old male with a past medical history significant for prior discectomy, who presents with back pain.

Lumbar strain.
• Medical Reasoning: He reports right-sided low back after moving a refrigerator approximately 5 days ago. X-ray of his lumbar spine is unremarkable. I do not believe this is related to his previous discectomy.
• Additional Testing: We will order a MRI of the lumbar spine for further evaluation.
• Medical Treatment: Initiate meloxicam 15 mg once daily, as well as Ultram 50 mg every 4 hours as needed.
• Specialist Referrals: We will refer him to physical therapy to be started after we get his MRI results back.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I advised the patient to discontinue the use of ibuprofen, but he may continue using Tylenol if he wishes.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports pain to palpation of the right lumbar spine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Pain to palpation is an examination finding elicited by the clinician, not a typical patient-reported symptom description.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports decreased flexion and extension of the back.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Decreased range of motion is usually documented as an exam finding; phrased as a patient report it is somewhat acceptable in HPI but more appropriate under Findings.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports a positive straight leg raise.
section_miscategorizations.reason: A positive straight leg raise is a specific physical exam maneuver result and should be documented as a finding, not as a patient-reported symptom.

section_miscategorizations.text: Examination reveals pain in the left hand.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This introduces a new symptom (left hand pain) that is not part of the documented chief complaint or back-pain-focused exam; without context it reads more like an additional complaint/history item than a focused finding for this visit.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient has been taking ibuprofen.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Current medication use belongs in the history/medication context, not in the exam/labs/imaging Findings section.
